GAP: no evidence in the material documents any UAE development aid or ODA flow to Jordan; the only Jordan-specific aid figure is the EU's €15.5 million allocation, and the material explicitly contains no direct UAE–Jordan bilateral aid claims.
GAP: no evidence in the material documents any UAE bilateral investment in Jordan (no deal, sector, amount or investor is cited for Jordan).

GAP: no evidence in the material documents any sovereign fund deployment (ADQ, Mubadala or ADFD) into Jordan; ADFD-linked activity in the material (ADEX AI for Development) is Africa-focused, not Jordan-specific.

GAP: no evidence in the material documents Masdar, DP World or AD Ports energy or infrastructure activity in Jordan; the only ports/energy partnership cited (AD Ports–Masdar offshore wind logistics) has no stated Jordan component.
GAP: no evidence in the material documents a UAE–Jordan remittance corridor (no worker, flow or volume data for Jordan is cited).

GAP: beyond Jordan's inclusion in the CEPA in-force list, no evidence in the material documents a UAE–Jordan diplomatic or strategic framework, official visit, joint statement or named official engagement.

The UAE and the European Commission maintain a working humanitarian-logistics dialogue, including a UAE–EU panel on global humanitarian supply-chain resilience co-hosted by DG ECHO in Brussels on 11 June 2026 with more than 80 senior representatives and Dubai Humanitarian participating.

Gaza is the largest recipient of UAE aid (~50% of all international aid entering Gaza), with more than $3 billion since 2023, 90,000+ tons delivered via 8,700 trucks, 80 airdrops and 13 ships across three delivery modes, and medical care provided to 75,000 Gazans.

UAE Gaza operations remained active through 2026: convoy operations passed the 300-convoy milestone (301st convoy carrying 290 tonnes), Operation Chivalrous Knight 3 delivered 416 tonnes via 47 trucks in July 2026, five convoys carried 792 tonnes on 12 July 2026, and Birds of Goodness airdrops cumulatively exceeded 3,924 tonnes.

The UAE announced a $30 million Sudan emergency response and pledged $500 million to a Sudan Humanitarian Fund in February 2026, amid confirmed famine in Sudan per the Global Report on Food Crises 2026.

The UAE provides direct bilateral food assistance to Kenya, sending 30 tonnes of food aid in February 2026 in response to La Niña-driven drought and deepening food insecurity; Kenya also co-chairs the Sudan de-escalation mechanism with the UAE, EU and Qatar.

The UAE and Qatar both co-signed the Gaza maritime corridor ministerial joint statement alongside the UK, US, Cyprus and the European Commission, and both co-chair the Sudan de-escalation ministerial mechanism with the EU and Kenya.

A 2024 academic study in The International Spectator comparatively analyses Qatar and the UAE as rival Gulf donors in Sudan, with Qatar operating institutionalized development assistance through QFFD in parallel to UAE agencies.
